questions.q: In which Indian state are the ruins of Nalanda Mahavihara located?
questions.explanation: Nalanda Mahavihara lies in the present-day state of Bihar in eastern India.

questions.q: What kind of institution was Nalanda Mahavihara?
questions.explanation: Nalanda was a great Buddhist monastery and centre of learning, often described as one of the world's earliest residential universities.

questions.q: With which religion was Nalanda Mahavihara primarily associated?
questions.explanation: Nalanda was a renowned Buddhist monastic university that drew students and monks from across Asia.

questions.q: Nalanda is best known today as the ruins of an ancient what?
questions.explanation: Nalanda Mahavihara is celebrated as one of the most famous ancient universities of the world.

questions.q: From around which century did Nalanda flourish as a centre of learning?
questions.explanation: Nalanda flourished as a major centre of learning from about the 5th century CE.

questions.q: In which broad region of India is Nalanda situated?
questions.explanation: Located in Bihar, Nalanda lies in the eastern part of India.

questions.q: What is the main building material seen in the surviving Nalanda ruins?
questions.explanation: The monasteries and temples of Nalanda were built largely of baked red brick, which still defines the ruins today.

questions.q: What largely happened to Nalanda Mahavihara, bringing its life as a centre of learning to an end?
options: It was abandoned after an earthquake in modern times
It was peacefully converted into a temple in the 1700s
It was flooded by the sea
questions.explanation: Nalanda was sacked and largely destroyed around 1200 CE, ending its long career as a great university.

questions.q: Nalanda Mahavihara was an example of what type of complex?
questions.explanation: Nalanda was a vast Buddhist monastic complex, combining residence halls (viharas) and temples (chaityas).

questions.q: Students and monks who studied at Nalanda came mainly from which part of the world?
questions.explanation: Nalanda attracted scholars from many parts of Asia, including China, Korea, Tibet and Southeast Asia.

questions.q: Under which imperial dynasty was Nalanda founded and given its first great patronage?
questions.explanation: Nalanda was founded and richly endowed by the Gupta emperors in around the 5th century CE.

questions.q: Which famous Chinese Buddhist pilgrim studied at Nalanda in the 7th century and left a detailed account of it?
questions.explanation: The Chinese monk Xuanzang (Hsuan-tsang) studied at Nalanda in the 7th century and described it vividly in his travelogue.

questions.q: Which later dynasty, devoted patrons of Buddhism, continued to support Nalanda after the Guptas?
questions.explanation: The Pala dynasty of Bengal and Bihar were major patrons of Nalanda in the later centuries of its existence.

questions.q: Another famous Chinese monk who studied at Nalanda and wrote about Indian Buddhism in the 7th century was:
questions.explanation: Yijing (I-tsing) visited and studied at Nalanda in the late 7th century and recorded its monastic life and curriculum.

questions.q: Besides Buddhist scriptures, what other subjects were taught at Nalanda?
options: Subjects such as logic, grammar, medicine and astronomy
questions.explanation: Nalanda offered a broad curriculum including logic, grammar, medicine, mathematics and astronomy alongside Buddhist philosophy.

questions.q: In 2016, the archaeological ruins of Nalanda Mahavihara received which international recognition?
options: They were inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site
They became a Formula One circuit
They were declared a free trade zone
They were turned into a national wildlife park
questions.explanation: The archaeological site of Nalanda Mahavihara was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2016.

questions.q: Which Turkic military commander is traditionally blamed for the sacking and destruction of Nalanda around 1200 CE?
questions.explanation: The general Bakhtiyar Khalji is traditionally held responsible for sacking Nalanda and its monasteries around 1200 CE.

questions.q: The word 'Mahavihara' in the name Nalanda Mahavihara essentially means:
questions.explanation: 'Mahavihara' means 'great monastery', reflecting Nalanda's status as a vast residential monastic complex.

questions.q: Visitors to the Nalanda ruins typically see rows of which two main structure types?
questions.explanation: The excavated site is laid out as a series of monastery buildings (viharas) facing a line of temples and stupas (chaityas).

questions.q: Roughly how long did Nalanda function as an important centre of learning before its destruction?
questions.explanation: Nalanda flourished from about the 5th century until around 1200 CE, a span of roughly seven to eight centuries.

questions.q: Which modern educational institution was established near the ruins to revive Nalanda's legacy?
questions.explanation: A modern Nalanda University was set up near the historic site to revive the ancient seat of learning, reopening to students in 2014.

questions.q: Which agency is responsible for the conservation of the excavated Nalanda ruins in India?
questions.explanation: The Archaeological Survey of India maintains and conserves the excavated remains of Nalanda Mahavihara.

questions.q: Which Gupta emperor is most commonly credited in tradition with founding Nalanda Mahavihara?
questions.explanation: Tradition and inscriptional evidence credit the Gupta ruler Kumaragupta I with founding Nalanda in the 5th century CE.

questions.q: Xuanzang reported that Nalanda housed a great library complex famously known by which name?
questions.explanation: Nalanda's celebrated library complex was known as the Dharmaganja, said to comprise several multi-storeyed buildings.

questions.q: According to tradition, three of the library buildings at Nalanda bore evocative names; which set below is associated with them?
questions.explanation: Tradition names the three library buildings as Ratnasagara, Ratnodadhi and Ratnaranjaka, evocatively rendered as 'Ocean of Jewels', 'Sea of Jewels' and 'Jewel-Adorned'.

questions.q: Which renowned Buddhist scholar associated with the Madhyamaka school is traditionally linked with Nalanda's intellectual heritage?
questions.explanation: Nagarjuna, the great Madhyamaka philosopher, is among the eminent scholars traditionally associated with Nalanda's lineage of learning.

questions.q: The most imposing surviving structure at Nalanda, a large stepped brick monument, is usually called:
options: The Great Stupa of Sariputra (Stupa No. 3)
questions.explanation: The towering brick monument known as Stupa No. 3, associated with the disciple Sariputra, is Nalanda's most prominent surviving structure.

questions.q: Which 19th-century surveyor played a key role in identifying the Nalanda ruins using Xuanzang's travel accounts?
questions.explanation: Alexander Cunningham, founder of the Archaeological Survey of India, helped identify Nalanda in the 19th century using the Chinese pilgrims' records.

questions.q: Nalanda lay in the ancient kingdom of Magadha, close to which other major Buddhist pilgrimage town?
questions.explanation: Nalanda is situated in ancient Magadha, near Rajgir (Rajagriha), an important early Buddhist and royal centre.

questions.q: Admission to study at Nalanda was famously selective, reportedly involving what?
options: Payment of a large gold fee only
A rigorous oral examination by gatekeeper scholars
questions.explanation: Accounts describe that aspiring students had to pass demanding oral examinations posed by learned gatekeepers before being admitted to Nalanda.
